title: What Your June 30 Financials Are Really Telling a Future Buyer
linkedin:
Your June 30 financials are being read by a future buyer. They just haven't told you yet.

When a buyer assesses a business, the first documents they request are the last three years of accounts. Most Australian owners prepare these for the ATO and move on. A buyer does not move on. They build a picture of what your profit is actually worth before any conversation about price.

The multiple is applied to normalised EBITDA, not the figure in your accounts. That normalised number removes owner-specific items: salary set for tax efficiency rather than at market rate, personal expenses run through the business, and genuine one-off costs that will not recur.

Here is where it becomes concrete. A business reporting $300,000 in profit might normalise to $420,000 with defensible add-backs applied. At a five-times multiple, that is a $600,000 difference in enterprise value. The question is whether the records are documented clearly enough to defend each adjustment in due diligence.

Buyers also reward three consistent patterns in the accounts: revenue growing steadily across three years, cash conversion that tracks the reported profit, and earnings that are believable without the owner present. Owner dependence is priced as risk, and the discount can be two to three times annual profit.

June 30 is still weeks away. Reviewing personal expenses running through the accounts, chasing aged receivables before year end, and documenting one-off costs with a brief management note shapes what a buyer reads in the accounts that matter most.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

google:
If you are a business owner in Queensland, NSW, or Victoria considering an eventual sale, the decisions you make before June 30 shape what a future buyer reads in the accounts that carry the most weight.

Buyers reviewing an Australian SME request three years of financial accounts. From those accounts, they build a normalised earnings figure, adjusting for owner salary set for tax efficiency, personal expenses run through the business, and genuine one-off costs. The multiple gets applied to that normalised figure, not the one in the accounts.

A business reporting $300,000 in profit might legitimately normalise to $420,000 with defensible add-backs. At a five-times multiple, that is a $600,000 difference in enterprise value. The quality of your record-keeping determines whether each adjustment can be defended in due diligence.

Several practical steps remain available before year end: reviewing any personal or lifestyle costs in the accounts, chasing aged receivables to present a stronger working capital position, and noting genuine one-off costs with a brief management comment so they are easy to explain later.

ProfitPulse works with owner-led businesses across the East Coast at this point in the cycle. If you want to understand what your June 30 financials are telling a future buyer, and what it would take to improve that picture, now is the right time to start.

Read the full post: https://profit-pulse.com.au/what-your-june-30-financials-are-really-telling-a-future-buyer
